hypotheses: Individuals under mortality salience are less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts.
Mortality salience mobilizes cognitive resources, suppressing thoughts of death out of focal attention.
claim.statement: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian by depleting cognitive resources.

data.source: Participants recruited on campus at the University of Toulouse
data.notes: Participants were assigned to mortality salience or control conditions; data were collected using moral dilemmas.

method.description: The study involved two experiments to test the effects of mortality salience on moral judgment, using moral dilemmas.
steps: Participants were randomly assigned to mortality salience or control (pain) condition.
Mortality salience manipulation involved writing about personal death or pain.
Participants judged the moral acceptability of actions in given scenarios.
Experiment 2 introduced varying levels of cognitive load before judging dilemmas.
method.independent_variables: Mortality salience condition, cognitive load level

results.summary: Mortality salience decreased utilitarian responses to moral dilemmas by depleting cognitive resources. The effect was comparable to a high cognitive load.

metadata.original_paper_title: Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
